diary:
6:20 rolled in like it always does on rainy Tuesdays—coffee first, then me squinting at the forecast like it might confess something new. I did my little Kirkland routine: quick garden check, soil feel, and a last look at the patio plants before the day fully turned into gray. By the time I headed toward Bothell, the SR-520 eastbound closure was already making the roads feel tense, so I left extra early and packed rain gear like I was going somewhere farther than UW Bothell. 

The Interdisciplinary Science Building felt warmer than it should, and I spent the morning digging through model assumptions, then drafting a short update for the team. Lunch in the dining hall was a relief—hot and quiet. After work, I grabbed staples at Safeway and even managed a couple pottery-supply errands in the brief lull. Back home, I tested clay on the pottery wheel, then later sat at Juanita Beach Park, listening to the rain soften the shoreline. Dinner at Kirkland Urban Village tasted like a small victory, and the whole day ended with me feeling oddly steady.
thought_process: I planned my day around the SR-520 closure automatically, like my brain has a built-in traffic model now. Leaving extra time meant I could focus at UW Bothell instead of fighting stress, and that mattered because my work today required careful attention to dataset assumptions. The drizzly weather also nudged me toward indoor tasks—meeting, lunch in, grocery staples—while I still carved out small outdoor moments at home when the rain eased. I chose the longer return route because calm commuting keeps me patient for the late afternoon pottery work, where small changes in clay behavior feel like they deserve a clear head. World events weren’t loud today, but the closing bridge and the persistent rain made everything feel slightly more fragile, which put me in a reflective mood—until the familiar rhythms of research, tools, and friends steadied me.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
